text:
*[Bug 18928] Missing pieces in the C API for environments*
<https://bugs.r-project.org/show_bug.cgi?id=18928>

--- Comment #21 from Lionel Henry (<mailto:lionel.hry@gmail.com|lionel.hry@gmail.com>) ---
Created attachment 3631
  --> <https://bugs.r-project.org/attachment.cgi?id=3631&action=edit>
Support base bindings in new API

We also need to implement support for base bindings for rlang, because we
retrieve values from the base environment at init time using our internal
`r_env_get()` helper which now uses the new API.

text:
*[Bug 18928] Missing pieces in the C API for environments*
<https://bugs.r-project.org/show_bug.cgi?id=18928>

--- Comment #22 from Lionel Henry (<mailto:lionel.hry@gmail.com|lionel.hry@gmail.com>) ---
Created attachment 3632
  --> <https://bugs.r-project.org/attachment.cgi?id=3632&action=edit>
Fail in `...elt()` with missing dots arguments

Fixes a small regression for this case:

```
f <- function(...) ...elt(1)
x <- 1
f(, 1)
```

On trunk this now returns the missing arg instead of failing.

with missing dots arguments

On trunk this now returns the missing arg instead of failing.

text:
*[Bug 19016] as.numeric("NA") throws an unnecessary coercion warning*
<https://bugs.r-project.org/show_bug.cgi?id=19016>

Elin Waring (<mailto:elin.waring@gmail.com|elin.waring@gmail.com>) changed:

           What    |Removed                     |Added
----------------------------------------------------------------------------
                 CC|                            |elin.waring@gmail.com

--- Comment #2 from Elin Waring (<mailto:elin.waring@gmail.com|elin.waring@gmail.com>) ---
I don't think that it is safe to assume that "NA" always is `NA`.  What if "NA"
represents North America, you would definitely want to know if it was coerced
to NA.
`as.numeric("")` also generates NA but without the warning.

text:
*[Bug 19016] as.numeric("NA") throws an unnecessary coercion warning*
<https://bugs.r-project.org/show_bug.cgi?id=19016>

--- Comment #3 from Dan Chaltiel (<mailto:dan.chaltiel@gmail.com|dan.chaltiel@gmail.com>) ---
(In reply to Elin Waring from comment #2)
Very interesting example, I had not thought about it. However, wouldn't it
apply to NaN and Inf as well, then? I could say that NaN represents "nanometer"
and Inf "infrared", but they are special cases, as Benjamin Tyner said.

Maybe the issue should be rephrased as "In a context of parsing to numeric, NA
should maybe be considered a special case in R, as Nan and Inf are special in
C++".

NA is a very special value in R and not in C++, so it would make a lot of sense
IMHO.

elements.text: [Bug 19016] as.numeric("NA") throws an unnecessary coercion warning

) ---
(In reply to Elin Waring from comment #2)
Very interesting example, I had not thought about it. However, wouldn't it
apply to NaN and Inf as well, then? I could say that NaN represents "nanometer"
and Inf "infrared", but they are special cases, as Benjamin Tyner said.

Maybe the issue should be rephrased as "In a context of parsing to numeric, NA
should maybe be considered a special case in R, as Nan and Inf are special in
C++".

NA is a very special value in R and not in C++, so it would make a lot of sense
IMHO.

text:
*[Bug 18743] "hclust()" documentation about "ward.D" and "ward.D2" need to be revised. In addition to the incorrect description of the "ward.D" and "ward.D2" options, there is a lack of correct information about the value of d to be set.*
<https://bugs.r-project.org/show_bug.cgi?id=18743>

--- Comment #7 from Atsushi Kimura (<mailto:atkimura@nstac.go.jp|atkimura@nstac.go.jp>) ---
Dear Martin Maechler,

Thank you for agreeing to my proposed revisions.

I frequently use the hclust function. I have researched various literatures
regarding parameter passing in Ward's method and the differences between the
ward.D and ward.D2 options, but I have encountered conflicting viewpoints,
which has been problematic. Therefore, I felt compelled to investigate the
implementation of Ward's method within the hclust function.

By creating a program for Ward's method analysis strictly adhering to Ward's
(1963) paper and comparing it with the input and output of the hclust function,
I confirmed the internal processing of the hclust function and recognized that
my proposed solution is correct. This work has been published in a Japanese
statistical journal:

Atsushi Kimura (2022), "Correct Method of Ward's Method Analysis Using the
hclust Function in R", ESTRELA 337, pp. 22-27 (2022) (in Japanese)

However, since the paper is in Japanese, it will only be accessible to those
who understand Japanese.


Therefore, I believe that improving the description in the online manual would
benefit many users of the hclust function, and I have submitted this suggestion
for revision.

Thank you for your consideration.

Atsushi Kimura
National Statistical Center, Japan

elements.text: [Bug 18743] "hclust()" documentation about "ward.D" and "ward.D2" need to be revised. In addition to the incorrect description of the "ward.D" and "ward.D2" options, there is a lack of correct information about the value of d to be set.

) ---
Dear Martin Maechler,

Thank you for agreeing to my proposed revisions.

I frequently use the hclust function. I have researched various literatures
regarding parameter passing in Ward's method and the differences between the
ward.D and ward.D2 options, but I have encountered conflicting viewpoints,
which has been problematic. Therefore, I felt compelled to investigate the
implementation of Ward's method within the hclust function.

By creating a program for Ward's method analysis strictly adhering to Ward's
(1963) paper and comparing it with the input and output of the hclust function,
I confirmed the internal processing of the hclust function and recognized that
my proposed solution is correct. This work has been published in a Japanese
statistical journal:

Atsushi Kimura (2022), "Correct Method of Ward's Method Analysis Using the
hclust Function in R", ESTRELA 337, pp. 22-27 (2022) (in Japanese)

However, since the paper is in Japanese, it will only be accessible to those
who understand Japanese.



Therefore, I believe that improving the description in the online manual would
benefit many users of the hclust function, and I have submitted this suggestion
for revision.

Thank you for your consideration.

Atsushi Kimura
National Statistical Center, Japan

text:
*[Bug 19016] as.numeric("NA") throws an unnecessary coercion warning*
<https://bugs.r-project.org/show_bug.cgi?id=19016>

--- Comment #4 from Elin Waring (<mailto:elin.waring@gmail.com|elin.waring@gmail.com>) ---
I think Comment 1 explains the there is actually an ISO standard that requires
the NaN and Inf treatment. 

There is not mention of these details in the `as.numeric()` documention.  The
only thing that is mentioned is that 

> Conversion does trim whitespace; non-numeric strings give NA + warning

So basically, "Inf" and "NaN" are treated as numerc strings.  "NA" is treated
as a non numeric string.

elements.text: [Bug 19016] as.numeric("NA") throws an unnecessary coercion warning

) ---
I think Comment 1 explains the there is actually an ISO standard that requires
the NaN and Inf treatment. 

There is not mention of these details in the

documention.  The
only thing that is mentioned is that
Conversion does trim whitespace; non-numeric strings give NA + warning

So basically, "Inf" and "NaN" are treated as numerc strings.  "NA" is treated
as a non numeric string.

text:
*[Bug 19021] New: history function does not work correctly*
<https://bugs.r-project.org/show_bug.cgi?id=19021>

            Bug ID: 19021
           Summary: history function does not work correctly
           Product: R
           Version: R 4.5.x
          Hardware: x86_64/x64/amd64 (64-bit)
                OS: Windows 64-bit
            Status: UNCONFIRMED
          Severity: normal
          Priority: P5
         Component: Windows GUI / Window specific
          Assignee: <mailto:R-core@R-project.org|R-core@R-project.org>
          Reporter: <mailto:jszhao@yeah.net|jszhao@yeah.net>

Hi there,

I'm using Rterm.exe on Windows. When I run help("rnorm") or history(), a
separate GUI window unexpectedly pops up. I assume this is because I'm using
Rterm (a terminal-based R interface), not Rgui.

If I run Rterm with the --ess flag, help("rnorm") displays the help page
directly in the terminal as expected. However, history() still produces an
error:

```
> history()
Error in file(con, "r") : cannot open the connection
In addition: Warning message:
In file(con, "r") :
  cannot open file 'R:/Temp\Rtmp8o3aOj\Rrawhist2adc60c6eab': No such file or
directory
```
When running R with the ESS (Emacs Speaks Statistics) package in Emacs on
Windows, I get the same error. On FreeBSD, the error message is different:
```
> history()
Error in .External2(C_savehistory, file): no history available to save
>
```

On Windows:
```
> sessionInfo()
R version 4.5.3 (2026-03-11 ucrt)
Platform: x86_64-w64-mingw32/x64
Running under: Windows 11 x64 (build 26200)

Matrix products: default
  LAPACK version 3.12.1

locale:
[1] LC_COLLATE=Chinese (Simplified)_China.utf8 
[2] LC_CTYPE=Chinese (Simplified)_China.utf8   
[3] LC_MONETARY=Chinese (Simplified)_China.utf8
[4] LC_NUMERIC=C                               
[5] LC_TIME=Chinese (Simplified)_China.utf8    

time zone: Asia/Shanghai
tzcode source: internal

attached base packages:
[1] stats     graphics  grDevices utils     datasets  methods   base     

loaded via a namespace (and not attached):
[1] compiler_4.5.3 tools_4.5.3
```

On FreeBSD:
```
> sessionInfo()
R version 4.5.2 (2025-10-31)
Platform: amd64-portbld-freebsd15.0
Running under: FreeBSD epyc 15.0-RELEASE-p4 FreeBSD 15.0-RELEASE-p4
releng/15.0-n281010-8ef0ed690df2 GENERIC amd64

Matrix products: default
LAPACK: /usr/local/lib/R/lib/libRlapack.so;  LAPACK version 3.12.1

locale:
[1] en_US.UTF-8/en_US.UTF-8/en_US.UTF-8/C/en_US.UTF-8/en_US.UTF-8

time zone: Asia/Shanghai
tzcode source: system (FreeBSD)

attached base packages:
[1] stats     graphics  grDevices utils     datasets  methods   base

loaded via a namespace (and not attached):
[1] compiler_4.5.2
```
